coredata.dc:description: Local bacterial infection remains an increasingly severe threat to human health worldwide, and infection control is still a challenging task in clinics. Systemic administration of antibiotics usually causes adverse effects and bacterial resistance. Multi-functions hydrogel without drug-resistance is highly desired. Herein, we first synthesize antibacterial carbon quantum dots (CQDAG) with low-drug resistance using gentamicin sulfate and diammonium citrate as precursors. Then carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS)-mixed CQDAG is reacted with oxidized dextran (ODex) through the Schiff base linkage to yield hydrogel network. Hydrogel decorated with 2 mg/mL CQDAG (CQDAG2-hydrogel) shows multi-functions including inherent self-healing, injectable, stretchable, compressive property, pH-dependent release of CQDAG and especially inhibiting biofilm formation, which are beneficial for treating bacterial infections. Besides, it is important that CQDAG2-hydrogel exhibits good cytocompatibility while sterilizing. In vivo antibacterial and wound healing evaluations demonstrate CQDAG2-hydrogel have the effect of anti-inflammation and promoting wound repair, which is manifested in wound healing rate, granulation tissue thickness and collagen disposition. The result indicates that the CQDAG2-hydrogel is excellent candidate for full-thickness skin wound healing.

head.ce:title: Low-drug resistance carbon quantum dots decorated injectable self-healing hydrogel with potent antibiofilm property and cutaneous wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Local bacterial infection remains an increasingly severe threat to human health worldwide, and infection control is still a challenging task in clinics. Systemic administration of antibiotics usually causes adverse effects and bacterial resistance. Multi-functions hydrogel without drug-resistance is highly desired. Herein, we first synthesize antibacterial carbon quantum dots (CQDAG) with low-drug resistance using gentamicin sulfate and diammonium citrate as precursors. Then carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS)-mixed CQDAG is reacted with oxidized dextran (ODex) through the Schiff base linkage to yield hydrogel network. Hydrogel decorated with 2 mg/mL CQDAG (CQDAG2-hydrogel) shows multi-functions including inherent self-healing, injectable, stretchable, compressive property, pH-dependent release of CQDAG and especially inhibiting biofilm formation, which are beneficial for treating bacterial infections. Besides, it is important that CQDAG2-hydrogel exhibits good cytocompatibility while sterilizing. In vivo antibacterial and wound healing evaluations demonstrate CQDAG2-hydrogel have the effect of anti-inflammation and promoting wound repair, which is manifested in wound healing rate, granulation tissue thickness and collagen disposition. The result indicates that the CQDAG2-hydrogel is excellent candidate for full-thickness skin wound healing.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin is the largest organ of the human body, protecting the human body from injury and microbial invasion, maintaining body fluids, electrolytes and nutrients [1]. Once there are serious defects in the skin, it will seriously affect people's health and life [2]. Wounds, especially large areas of full-thickness wounds, need to be repaired for a long time [3]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for an ideal wound dressing which can heal the wound quickly, promote wound healing and reduce scar formation [4]. At present, rubber, foam, membrane, hydrogel and other wound dressings have been developed, in which hydrogel dressings can maintain a moist wound environment, absorb tissue exudates, make oxygen permeate, cool wounds and alleviate the pain of patients [5]. Especially, injectable hydrogel dressings have unique properties, such as in situ coated drugs, filling wounds (or even irregular spaces), gluing wounds, and so on [6]. However, although injectable hydrogel dressings can fill wounds, stick to wounds, and protect wounds, most hydrogels do not have strong elasticity, which would result in some deformation and external force injury [7]. This damage can shorten the service life of hydrogel and cause infection. The hydrogel with self-healing ability opens up a new way for the safety and durability of the product [8,9].

Besides, damaged tissue is susceptible to microbial infections during wound surgery, which can lead to inflammation and tissue infections in the body [10]. For example, microbes such as E. coli and S. aureus can destroy normal tissue, and sometimes the host's immune system can't fight them off [11]. Bacteria proliferate quickly and form biofilm in the wound site, which is more difficult to be removed [12]. To solve this problem, hydrogel loaded with antibiotic is a common choice for surgical infections [13]. It has significant advantages, such as local delivery and sustained effective load release, which result in increased bioavailability and minimal antibiotic cytotoxicity [14]. Although adding antibiotics to hydrogels is effective against infection, the overuse of antibiotics can lead to acquired bacterial resistance [15]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for an antibacterial and antibiofilm hydrogel without concern of drug resistance.

Nanotechnology brings an alternative strategy to relieve the pressure of drug resistance for antibacterial hydrogel because of multiple antimicrobial modes [16]. Metal and metal oxide nanoparticles have been introduced into hydrogel for wound therapy with good antibacterial activity [17,18]. Nevertheless, the use of metal or metal oxide nanomaterials may cause harm to human health and the environment [19]. Carbon nanomaterials have perfect biocompatibility and show distinctive antibacterial activity [20-23]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), zero-dimensional carbon material and the newest member of the carbon-based material family, have also been shown highly antibacterial and antibiofilm effects [24]. The possible antibacterial mechanism is attributed to the highly positive charge, which causes serious disruption of the bacterial membrane structures [25]. The endogenous ROS induced by CQDs is another important antibacterial mode because of their oxidation effect to bacterial physiology in many ways [26]. Previously reports have been shown CQDs can enhance the mechanical strength of chitosan hydrogel [27]. Three-dimensional graphene hydrogel-loaded CQDs has been applied in the domain of biosensors and bioelectronics to precisely detect the concentration of E. coli [28]. These findings suggest that CQDs can give hydrogel benefits. However, as far as we know, there is no CQDs-hydrogel composites been prepared for antibiofilm and anti-inflammation, which limits the application of the CQDs served as bioactive components in the field of antibacterial hydrogel.

As our previous report, CQDs derived from gentamicin sulfate can combat antibiotic resistance and eradicate mature biofilms, which simultaneously possess good biocompatibility [29]. On this basis, in this study, we proposed a facile approach to develop and design a kind of low-drug resistance antibacterial injectable hydrogel wound dressing with self-healing property via a Schiff base linkage between CMCS and ODex, which is decorated with bactericidal CQDAG derived from gentamicin sulfate and ammonium citrate dibasic. The CQDAG could not induce bacterial resistance, thus the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel can be used to treat bacteria-related infection without worrying about drug-resistance. The chemical structure, morphology, swelling ratio, rheological properties and gel time of the CQDAG-hydrogel are systematically evaluated. The CQDAG2-hydrogel dressings have rapid self-healing ability and good injectability. In addition, it also shows high cell selectivity, which is manifested in excellent antibacterial activity and good cytocompatibility. Furthermore, the therapeutic effect of the CQDAG2-hydrogel dressing on a full-thickness skin defect model is explored by observation of the wound healing process and histological analysis.

Ammonium citrate dibasic (C6H14N2O7, ACD) was obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Gentamicin sulfate came from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Dextran70 (Mw = 70000, Dex), sodium periodate (NaIO4), glycol and carboxymethyl chitosan (substitution degree ≥ 80%, CMCS) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Other reagents, such as potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), etc., were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The above agents were utilized to experiments without further treatment. Nanjing Lezhen Biotechnology Co., Ltd. provided us with Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923, S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922, E. coli). Acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) dye was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. A commercial antibacterial hydrogel dressing (containing antimicrobial agent, silver ion) was applied to infected wound on the back of rat, which was bought from Shenzhen Ai Jiete Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. And double-distilled water was used in the whole experiment.

The nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were obtained by NMR spectrometer (AVANCEIII 500 MHz, Bruker, Germany). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of CQDs images was received by a JEM-2100 electron microscope at 200 kV (JEOL Ltd, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of materials was evaluated by a VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer. The fluorescence (FL) spectra of the CQDAG were recorded on a FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Hydrogel and biofilm images were performed on a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) TSM-7500F (JEOL Ltd. Japan) at 10.0 kV. The compression modulus of the hydrogel (diameter: 15 mm, height: 10 mm) were measured in a 500 N load tank at room temperature using the US Instron5943 universal mechanical device. The hydrogel was compressed to the yield point at a displacement rate of 1 mm/min under unconstrained conditions. Rheological measurement of the hydrogel was carried out using a Modular Compact Rheometer (MCR102, Anton Paar, Austria). Fluorescence images of confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) were obtained to observe the viability and death of bacteria in biofilm. UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out by a Biomate 3S spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) analysis was conducted on an UltrafleXtreme MALDI-TOF mass spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). The Zeta potential (ζ) of CQDAG was performed by a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK).

In this study, CQDAG was prepared by two-step method. The first step was to prepare CQDs from ACD (CQDACD). 1 g ACD solid powder was accurately weighed in 20 mL crucible. 5 mL deionized water was added to dissolve the raw material, and then put into muffle furnace. The parameter was set to 180 ℃ for 2 h. The calcined black solid was removed and dissolved in 5 mL deionized water for 1 h. Then the black solution was centrifuged at 20,000 r/min for 30 min. The supernatant was put into the dialysis bag (MWCO = 3000 Da) against the double-distilled water. Finally, the excess impurities or bacteria in the dialysis solution were removed by using a membrane filter (0.22 μm). The filtered solution was freeze-dried and stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃. Next step, the surface of CQDACD would be modified by gentamicin. 50 mg CQDACD and 50 mg gentamicin sulfate were mixed in crucible and dissolved with 1 mL water under the help of ultrasonic dissolution. It was calcined in a muffle furnace with the parameter of 180 ℃ for 2 h. The processes to get gentamicin-CQDACD (CQDAG) were the same as the corresponding experimental procedures in the first step.

4 g Dextran70 (MW ~ 70000) and 3.4 g sodium periodate were measured accurately followed by dissolving into 50 mL double-distilled water under help of magnetic stirring. The mixture was under the condition of darkness. At room temperature, the oxidation reaction was carried out for 24 h, and the solution was slightly yellow. Then 1 g ethylene glycol was reacted with the mixture for 2 h to terminate the further oxidation of dextran. The solution was then dialyzed against water in the dialysis bag (MWCO: 8000 ~ 14000) until it is colorless. Finally, the powder product (ODex) was obtained by freeze-drying the dialyzed solution. 2 g ODex powders were add into 20 mL phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) to make 10% (w/v) ODex solution. The oxidation degree of dextran was determined by 5-hydroxytryptamine colorimetric titration [17]. In general, 40 mg ODex powders were dissolved in 8 mL of hydroxylamine hydrochloride solution (0.25 M). 0.05% methyl orange was used as pH indicator and the mixed solution was titrated with 0.1 M NaOH. The pH of the solution is recorded. Taking the volume of NaOH as the x-axis, the pH curve was drawn. According to the volume of the consumed NaOH solution (ΔV), the formula for calculating the oxidation degree of ODex was (ΔV × 0.001 × nNaOH/0.02) × 162/2, in which 162 is the molar mass of dextran monomer.

A certain amount of CQDAG was mixed with 2 mL 4% (w/v) CMCS PBS, and then 10% (w/v) ODex was added. With the help of vortex mixer, the mixture was uniformly dispersed and poured into the mold at 37 ℃. The concentrations of CQDAG in the hydrogel were 1 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL, 3 mg/mL and 4 mg/mL, respectively. After gel for 30 min, the properties of the hydrogels were tested. The hydrogel can be made into different shapes for various experiments.

The hydrogel was made into a column (15 mm in diameter and 10 mm in height) and tested with a universal mechanical testing machine after 30 min of gelation. The constant displacement rate was 1 mm/min. When the compressive stress reaches the yield point and the internal fracture of hydrogel occurs, the test was completed. The stress–strain curves were plotted and the compression modulus in the linear strain range were calculated.

The gel time of hydrogel was determined by tilt method. First, a certain amount of CQDAG and CMCS solution were mixed in a 10 mL glass bottle. Then, timing started when ODex solution was injected to the mixture. The glass bottle was gently shaking so that the solution is evenly mixed. The tilt angle of the glass bottle was constantly changed to observe the flow of the mixture. When the mixture cannot flow, timing was stopped.

Three different methods of rheological measurement for hydrogel were carried out by Rheometer (MCR102, Anton Paar, Austria): (1) the storage modulus of hydrogel was measured by variable angle frequency scanning method; (2) the critical strain of hydrogel was measured by strain amplitude scanning method; and (3) the self-healing ability of hydrogel was measured by alternating step strain scanning method. Macroscopic self-healing experiment was also performed by process of chopping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the released CQDAG from the hydrogel matrix
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, the standard curve of CQDAG needs to be determined. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of different concentrations of CQDAG solution were measured by a spectrophotometer. The absorbances at the absorption peak were linearly fitted with the corresponding concentration. Then, the standard curve and the equation were obtained. Next, the CQDAG-hydrogels were separately immersed in 20 mL pH 7.4 or pH 5.5 PBS. The mixtures were subsequently put into a shaker under 37 ℃. At each fixed time (0 h, 1 h, 3.75 h, 7.15 h, 16 h, 1 d, 2 d, 4 d, 6 d, 8 d, 10 d), 2 mL solution was aspirated with a pipette gun, and its absorbance was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Then 2 mL fresh PBS was added to the mixture to keep the total volume unchanged. According to the measured absorbance, the concentration of CQDAG was calculated by the equation of standard curve. Then, the percentage of the released CQDAG was obtained. Finally, the relationship between the percentage of the released CQDAG and the corresponding time was plotted into curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial and antibiofilm experiments of CQDAG and CMCS/ODex-CQDAG-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial resistance to CQDAG was tested. Firstly, according to the method reported earlier [30], minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of CQDAG against S. aureus and E. coli were measured. On this basis, the bacterial suspension (1 × 106 CFU/mL) containing 1/2 MIC gentamicin or CQDAG was used for the next determination of MICs. The test was repeated each day for 28 passages.

Bacteriostatic zone experiments were used to evaluate the antibacterial properties of hydrogels. S. aureus and E. coli were served as model bacteria. Briefly, a hydrogel with a diameter of 15 mm and a height of 2 mm was placed in the center of an LB agar plate with spreading 100 μL of fresh bacterial suspension (106 ~ 107 CFU/mL). Subsequently, the bacteria were cultured at 37 ℃ for 18 h. The size of the area without bacterial colonies was measured to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the hydrogel.

Assessment of antibacterial activity of the hydrogels was conducted by a direct contact test [31]. In brief, 200 μL of 4% CMCS/CQDAG and 50 μL of 10% ODex solution (PBS, pH 7.4) was added to the 48-well plate. After gelation for 30 min, a 20 μL bacterial suspension (in LB, 106 CFU/mL) was added to the surface of the hydrogel. Next, the hydrogels are placed in an incubator at 37 ℃ for 24 h. Then, 1 mL of sterilized PBS was added to each well to re-suspend any bacteria alive. Hydrogel without CQDAG was used as control group. After incubation at 37 ℃ for 18 ~ 24 h, colonies on the LB plates were counted. Experiment for each group was repeated for three times. The killing rates of hydrogel against bacteria were calculated according to the following formula:

SEM was utilized to observe the biofilm after the cultivation of 20 μL S. aureus suspension (in LB, 1 × 106 CFU/mL) on the surface of the hydrogel. The bacterial morphology was immobilized with 2 mL 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 24 h. The excess glutaraldehyde was then removed and washed with PBS (pH 7.4) for three times, followed by gradient dehydration with 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95% and 100% ethanol. Finally, after being freeze-dried and sprayed with gold, the bacterial morphology on the surface of hydrogel was observed under SEM.

After 24 h cultivation of 20 μL S. aureus suspension (in LB, 106 CFU/mL), the formed biofilm on the surface of the hydrogel was stained with AO/EB and observed by CLSM to evaluate the Live/Dead state of bacteria.

The biocompatibility of hydrogel with mouse fibroblasts (3 T3) was determined by a direct contact test. The sterilized CMCS, CQDAG and ODex were poured into the Petri dish. The hydrogel with a thickness of 1 mm was formed in a CO2 incubator at 37 ℃. Then the hydrogel was cut into a disc with a diameter of 5 mm by a micro perforator. 10% fetal bovine serum, Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin were used as complete growth medium. 3 T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates with a density of 5000 cells/well. When the 3 T3 cell adheres to the wall, the hydrogel disk is placed in the well. After incubation for 1, 2 and 3 days, the hydrogels were taken out. MTT test was used to evaluate cell proliferation and viability. 10 μL MTT (5 mg/mL) was mixed with 100 μL medium in each well. The 96-well plate was then placed in the incubator containing 5% CO2 and cultured for 4 h at 37 ℃. Then, 100 μL suspension was transferred from each well to another 96-wells plate. The blue formazan formed by the reaction between MTT and mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 200 μL). Finally, the absorbance of formazan was read at 540 nm using a Thermo Scientific Micro plate Reader. The cells cultured in complete medium were used as control. The experiment was repeated for six times. Cell adhesion and survival were observed by a fluorescent inverted microscope (IX 53, Olympus).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial assay and wound healing evaluation
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the antibacterial effect of CQDAG-hydrogel in vivo, back wound model of rat was established. Fifteen male rats were randomly divided into a control group (hydrogel formed by CMCS and ODex only), a commercial hydrogel dressing group, and a CQDAG2-hydrogel group. The wound was about 10 mm in diameter. The wounds were infected with 100 μL of S. aureus (1 × 109 CFU/mL). Then 500 μL of the above three hydrogels were separately injected on the wound surfaces by syringes. After 24 h, the number of bacteria around the wound tissue was measured. The wound tissue was homogenized with sterile saline. 100 μL of the homogenate was spread on LB solid medium. The growing colonies were counted after cultured for 24 h. The process of wound healing was regularly observed (3, 5, 7, 10 and 14 days) and photographed. The wound area was measured by depicting the wound boundary on a drawing sheet. The following formula was used to calculate wound contraction (%):
Wound

Area ( 0 d a y )

where “n” stands for the day, such as 0, 3, 5, 7, 10 and 14.

To further study of wound healing at 14 days after the operation, the wound and adjacent normal skin tissue samples were histologically tested after fixed with a 10% formaldehyde solution. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was used to analyze specimens containing dermis and subcutaneous tissue for histological testing, and Masson’s trichrome staining was used to examine collagen formation during healing. This study complies with the guidelines of the animal care and use committee.

All the values were represented by mean ± SD. All the records were obtained from at least three free-running tests. According to the TTEST function in Microsoft Excel, the double tail heteroscedasticity t-test was used to evaluate the statistical significance. When the P value was<0.05, the difference between the groups was statistically significant.

Many biochemical processes in wound healing are very complicated [33]. Therefore, a dressing with multifunctional properties is very desirable in promoting wound healing. In this study, we designed a self-healing injectable CQDAG-hydrogel dressing with excellent antibacterial and anti-biofilm activities accompanied by low drug resistance, good cytocompatibility and non-hemolysis, which would be helpful to promote wound healing. The synthesis process of hydrogel dressing is displayed in Scheme 1 . First of all, CMCS is mixed with CQDAG, which act as an antibacterial active substance. The mixture is further reacted with ODex, which is obtained from dextran oxidized by sodium periodate, through the Schiff base linkage between primary amine and aldehyde, yielding the black CQDAG-hydrogel. The oxidation degree of dextran is 15%.

In order to study the chemical reactions occurring in the synthesis of the hydrogels, we shall first characterize the dextran and ODex. Fig. S1 exhibits the 1H NMR spectrums of Dex and ODex. The proton peaks on aldehyde groups appeared at 9.18 and 9.64 ppm, indicating that the ODex is successfully prepared. Furthermore, compared with dextran, the infrared spectrum of ODex has an additional peak at 1721 cm−1 (Fig. S2), which is the characteristic peak of aldehyde group, indicating that hydroxyl on the molecular chain are oxidized to aldehyde group after selective oxidation of dextran by sodium periodate. The characteristic absorption peak of CN generated by Schiff base reaction in hydrogels overlaps with CO peak and fails to accurately produce CN peak. The peak at 1586 cm−1 is wider than that of CMCS, indicating that the Schiff base reaction take place and the hydrogel is formed [34]. However, there is no characteristic absorption peak of aldehyde group in the formed CQDAG-hydrogel, which means that Schiff base reaction is complete. Besides, FTIR spectrums (Fig. S3) show a broad peak in the range of 3600–3100 cm−1, which can be assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H groups on the surface of CQDAG. CQDAG and gentamicin have the same absorption peaks at 1611 cm−1 and 873 cm−1, which are respectively attributed to the bending vibration and out of plane deformation vibration of N–H on amino group [35]. Moreover, the high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S4) is divided into two peaks, where the peak position at 400.0 eV is owing to N–H. These results demonstrate that it contains amino groups on the surface of CQDAG, so it can participate in Schiff base reaction. Fig. S5 also shows that pure CQDAG powders can crosslinked with ODex without CMCS. It has been reported in the literature that the bond of Schiff base will be broken in a mildly acidic environment [36]. Therefore, the linkage between CQDAG and ODex in the hydrogels would degrade and then release CQDAG in the bacterial or biofilm microenvironment. Significantly, as we have previously reported, CQDs derived from gentamicin sulphate do not develop bacterial resistance and can effectively destroy mature biofilms [29]. This would eliminate clinical concerns about bacterial resistance during bacteria and biofilm-related infections, especially skin wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures of the CQDAG and CQDAG-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporation of nanoparticles will inevitably affect the properties of hydrogels. In order to obtain hydrogels with good comprehensive performance, the hydrogels with different CQDAG concentrations (0, 1, 2, 3, 4 mg/mL) are prepared (Fig. S6) and their application properties are also evaluated. The hydrogels are termed CQDAG0-hydrogel, CQDAG1-hydrogel, CQDAG2-hydrogel, CQDAG3-hydrogel and CQDAG4-hydrogel, respectively. First of all, the morphology of CQDAG is characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM). It is observed that the CQDAG is ultra-small and generally mono-disperse with average size of 3.73 nm (Fig. 1 A), which would facilitate CQDAG decorated into the hydrogel network. The inset image at bottom right-hand corner displays a lattice spacing distance of 0.23 nm, which is similar to graphite (100) facets [37]. Moreover, the FL spectra of CQDAG (Fig. S7) exhibit red-shift in emission peaks when excitation wavelengths increase from 300 to 500 nm, which indicated the fluorescence emission of CQDAG is dependent on the excitation wavelength. Fig. S8 displays photographs of CQDAG solution under ambient light and 365 nm light. After decorated CQDAG (Fig. S6), the structure of hydrogel is observed by SEM. As can be seen in Fig. 1B ~ D, the three hydrogels show highly porous and interconnected structures. Besides, as the CQDAG content increased, the pore size is found to decrease. However, CQDAG4-hydrogel has no obvious porous structure, which may be due to pore shrinkage during freeze-drying (Fig. 1E) [38]. It indicates that the crosslinking density is higher after adding CQDAG. Considering the application of tissue engineering to promote cell proliferation, oxygen and nutrient transport, this interconnected porous structure is an important factor [39]. The hydrogels have a certain capacity to absorb liquids and can cause their own volume to expand. When used for dressing, hydrogels can absorb the tissue fluid from the wound and maintain the moist environment to prevent the loss of tissue fluid [40]. With the increasing of CQDAG content, the swelling ratio of the hydrogel decreases (Fig. 1F). The swelling ratio of CQDAG0-hydrogel is 36.8 within 24 h, and reached saturation after 12 h. Compared with pure gel, when the CQDAG concentration increased from 1 mg/mL to 4 mg/mL, the swelling ratio decreased from 31.8 to 13.6. The possible reason is that CQDAG affects the porosity of the hydrogel structure, thereby reducing the water absorption capacity of the hydrogel. The frequency scanning method is used to test the rheological properties of CQDAG-hydrogels, and their viscoelastic properties are studied in a wide frequency range (0.01 to 100 Hz). As shown in Fig. 1G, all hydrogels exhibit similar nonlinear rheological behavior. With the increase of the amount of CQDAG, the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G'') increase. G' is about one order of magnitude larger than G'' over the entire frequency sweep range, which indicates the stability of the three-dimensional network in the gel system through the Schiff base reaction [41]. Besides, the loss tangent of hydrogels (Fig. S9) is improved with the increase of CQDAG concentration. It implies that the addition of CQDAG could slightly restrict the mobility of hydrogel. However, these values of loss tangent are all small enough, which means that the hydrogels have good viscosity properties. In addition, it is also noted that the viscoelasticity of the hydrogel becomes less effective at high CQDAG concentrations than that of low CQDAG concentrations. This signifies that the saturation of the nanomaterials will have an effect on the structure of hydrogel. As nanoparticles, CQDAG can strengthen the resistance to compression (Fig. 1H). The effect is also displayed in compression modulus (Fig. 1I). It may be due to many cross-linking sites of nanoparticles [42] and forces formed by CQDAG, such as covalent bond, hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces [43]. However, there are balance between compressive strength and deformation capacity, that both high hardness and low deformability of hydrogels are prone to breakage during application.

Therefore, it is necessary to choose hydrogel with suitable concentration of CQDAG. CQDAG2-hydrogel simultaneously possesses high compressive strength and deformation ability, which is suitable for hydrogel dressing. Besides, the gel time of hydrogel is of great significance for its practical application in the field of biomedicine. When the CQDAG concentration increased from 0 to 4 mg/mL, the average gel time decreased from 60 s to 41 s (Fig. S10). Nevertheless, all gel time in this study would suitable for injectable hydrogel applications (Fig. S11). We choose CQDAG2-hydrogel as candidate to evaluate other practical performances for wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bending, pressing and self-healing properties of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: When the hydrogel is applied to the wound site, it will inevitably be interfered by external forces such as bending and extrusion. CQDAG2-hydrogel has flexible property that make them resistant to bending and pressing (Fig. 2 A), due to the flexible polymer chains of CMCS/ODex and the dynamically chemical bonded hydrogel networks [44]. Importantly, it is need for self-healing property during treatment of wound therapy, which could prolong lifespan of hydrogel. To investigate the self-healing ability, a rheological recovery experiment is performed with the CQDAG2-hydrogel. First of all, a strain amplitude sweep assay is carried out to obtain the critical point of the hydrogel in a solid–liquid state (Fig. 2B). At about 300% strain, the storage modulus curve intersects with the loss modulus curve, which is related to the critical point. When the strain exceeds 300%, the value of G′ drops sharply and is lower than the value of G′′, indicating the destruction of the hydrogel network [45]. Subsequently, the rheological recovery behavior of the CQDAG2-hydrogel is performed using a continuous step strain method. As shown in the Fig. 2C, when the hydrogel is subjected to a strain of 300%, the value of G' immediately drops from 11441 Pa to 8188 Pa. Nevertheless, the value of G' returns to the original level once the strain returns to 0.1%. The repeated fracture and recovery behaviors indicate that the hydrogel has fast and efficient self-healing ability. Next, we further evaluate the self-healing ability of the hydrogel from a macro perspective (Fig. 2D). Firstly, the cylindrical CQDAG2-hydrogel is cut into four segments. Immediately, the six surfaces contacted each other one by one, making it self-heal for 3 h at 37 °C under a certain humidity condition. The boundaries among the segments became blurred. Besides, it could be lifted from one end. In addition, the healed columnar hydrogel maintain integrity when bent into a U-shape. The results show that the hydrogel has good self-healing ability and efficiency. The dynamic covalent Schiff base network between the amine group of CMCS/CQDAG and the formaldehyde group of ODex gives the fast and automatic self-healing characteristics of hydrogels under physiological conditions [46]. It can be considered as a quasi-covalent bond due to the intrinsic kinetic equilibrium of the Schiff base [47]. Therefore, the cleavage and regeneration of the imine bond has always existed in the hydrogel network [48,49]. Besides, CQDAG could also promote the self-healing process, which can be attributed to hydrogen bonding between the surface functional groups of the CQDAG and Van der Waals bonding induced between adjacent CQDAG [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of CQDAG2-hydrogel and release of CQDAG in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The degradability of antibacterial hydrogel dressing is critical to tissue repair and regeneration. Hydrogels that degrade too slow or too fast can lead to failure of tissue repair and also affect the release of antimicrobial agents [34]. Fig. 3 A and 3B show the curves of the degradation behaviors of the CQDAG0-hydrogel and CQDAG2-hydrogel in pH 5.5 and 7.4 PBS. The hydrogels display obvious water absorption and swelling properties in the early stage, especially in the former 24 h. And the swelling slowly reaches equilibrium. With the prolongation of soaking time, the degradation of the hydrogels begins to hold dominant position. Then, the hydrogels lose weight, which is mainly due to the collapse and decomposition of the structure caused by the internal cross-link breakage. For pH 7.4, it can be seen that the two hydrogels are completely degraded on the 10th days and 14 days respectively. The degradation rate of CQDAG0-hydrogel is fast, which will cause great inconvenience in drug delivery and tissue therapy. CQDAG2-hydrogel shows relatively slow degradation rate, which is beneficial to long-term treatment, especially to bacteria-related infections. It is reasonable that the incorporated CQDAG delay the degradation rate of the hydrogel because of multi-crosslinking sites on the surface of nanoparticles [50]. For pH 5.5, CQDAG0-hydrogel and CQDAG1-hydrogel are totally degraded respectively on the 8th day and 9th day. Additionally, the SEM images (Fig. 3C) of CQDAG2-hydrogel after 3 days of degradation still show the pore structure, while the images show a great collapsed structure at 7th day, especially in pH 5.5 environment, which indicate that the hydrogel degrades quickly under acidic conditions. It agrees with the degradation curve of CQDAG2-hydrogel. Then the release of CQDAG in the hydrogel is investigated at pH 7.4 and pH 5.5 respectively. As shown in Fig. 3D and Fig. S12, the release rate of CQDAG is faster under acidic condition than under neutral condition. After CQDAG2-hydrogel is immersed in pH 5.5 PBS for 2 days, about 21.4% of CQDAG is released, while the value is 20.6% when treated with pH 7.4 PBS. Over time, this phenomenon became more and more obvious. The release of CQDAG comes from two parts. First, CQDAG exists in the hydrogel in a physically mixed form. This part of CQDAG can be quickly released in a solution environment. Second, CQDAG exists in covalent bonds in the hydrogel. The release rate of this part of CQDAG is relatively slow, which is due to the acid instability of Schiff base. Therefore, the released percentage of CQDAG is higher at pH 5.5. Importantly, it is generally known that the more severe the bacterial infection of the wound, the more obvious the acidic environment. Thus, not only the physically mixed CQDAG but also the CQDAG involved in the Schiff base will be released. The acidity is weak in the site where the bacterial infection is not serious. CQDAG would be released slowly, so as to achieve release on-demand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial and antibiofilm activities of CQDAG and CQDAG2-hydrogel

ce:section.ce:para: Since bacterial infection can increase the formation of exudate and delay the process of wound healing, antibacterial activity is a needful property of hydrogel dressing [51]. In this part, we will explore the surface antibacterial and anti-biofilm properties of CQDAG2-hydrogel. First of all, the bacterial resistance to CQDAG is carried out (Fig. 4 A and 4B). Compared with gentamicin, CQDAG would be less prone to induce S. aureus and E. coli resistances in 28 passages. CQDAG can destroy mature S. aureus biofilm more easily than that of gentamicin (Fig. S13). After decorated with CQDAG, the hydrogel possesses antibacterial activity. It is shown in the bacteriostatic zone experiment (Fig. S14). The sizes of inhibition zones for CQDAG-hydrogel against S. aureus and E. coli are respectively 3.2 mm and 2.9 mm. Nevertheless, there are no bacterial inhibition zones for pure hydrogel. The contact antimicrobial assay is also used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 4C, for S. aureus and E. coli, the CQDAG2-hydrogel respectively results in a 2.3log10 and 2log10 reduction in the number of CFU, while the two strains of bacteria on the surface of CQDAG0-hydrogel separately grow to 108.778 and 108.903 CFU/mL. The images of S. aureus and E. coli colonies (Fig. S15) agree with the results of the contact antimicrobial assay. Besides, as shows in Fig. 4D, S. aureus after incubation for 24 h on the surface of the CQDAG0-hydrogel can form a mature biofilm. However, there is no bacterial aggregation on the surface of CQDAG2-hydrogel, just a small number of planktonic bacteria remains. The CLSM images are further performed to investigate the Live/Dead state of S. aureus (Fig. 4E). The green color stands for live bacteria while the red color is the symbol of dead bacteria. We can see that there are almost no dead bacteria in the biofilm treated with pure hydrogel and a 16 μm thickness of mature biofilm is formed on the surface. However, after treated with CQDAG2-hydrogel, bacteria become inactive, which demonstrates a good performance to inhibit biofilm formation (93.6%).

First of all, MALDI-TOF MS is first carried out to investigate the active-structure preservation of CQDAG. The MALDI-TOF MS spectra of CQDAG (Fig. S16A and Fig. S16B) after gel chromatography purification show some features in debris of gentamicin. For example, peaks at m/z 332.2 and 304.2 could be assigned to preserved fragmentation of gentamicin (Fig. S16D). Therefore, we deduce that CQDAG can possess superior antibacterial activity by preserving the active structure of the starting materials. The zeta potential of CQDAG is measured and display positive value in pH 7.4 (+3.19 mV) and pH 5.5 (+9.32 mV) solutions (Fig. S17). As we know, bacterial membrane is negatively charged and the microenvironment of biofilm is mildly acidic. Thus, we infer that the cationic property of the CQDAG is one of the reasons for their antibacterial and antibiofilm activities. The cationic membrane-disrupting mechanisms could reduce the risk of developing resistance [52], as these interactions with bacteria are based on physical charge–charge interactions and so lack defined membrane targets [53]. Furthermore, the intracellular ROS generated by CQDAG are indirectly measured by fluorescence of 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF). The intensity of the DCF fluorescence is proportional to the content of intracellular ROS (Fig. S18). We find that CQDAG can generate intracellular ROS in bacterial cells. The relative contents of ROS induced by CQDAG (100 μg/mL) are even greater than that of H2O2 (1 mM), which served as a positive control group. ROS have a strong oxidation ability, which effectively inhibits bacterial growth [54]. Therefore, we speculate that the intracellular ROS generated by the CQDAG are reasonable for the antibacterial activity. Due to the positive charge property and the ability to produce intracellular ROS, CQDAG would not be completely bound specifically to bacteria, as in antibiotics during the process of sterilization. Thus, CQDAG hardly causes bacterial resistance.

In addition, bacteria can form a protective biofilm that acts as a barrier against host immune responses and provides resistance against antibiotics [55]. After making gentamicin into nanoparticles, the physicochemical interactions between CQDAG and extracellular polymeric substances of the biofilm matrix could be enhanced, which makes it easy for CQDAG to enter the biofilm (Fig. S13). Then CQDAG can eradicate mature biofilm by multiple antibacterial modes.

Biomaterials with high biocompatibility are important for practical applications [56]. We evaluated the compatibility of hydrogels with mammalian cells in vitro. The blood compatibility of the hydrogel is first evaluated by exposing RBCs to the surface of the hydrogel (Fig. 5 A). After cultivated for 1 h, the absorbance of hemoglobin is detected by an UV–Vis spectrophotometer. We can see that both CQDAG0-hydrogel and CQDAG2-hydrogel showed 0% hemolytic activity (Fig. 5B). This demonstrate that the prepared hydrogel is hemo-compatible to RBCs. For better evaluation, RBCs are also imaged by a microscope (Fig. 5C) after exposure to the hydrogel surface. It is observed in the groups of PBS and CQDAG2-hydrogel that the cells had a circular morphology, which is typical feature of healthy RBCs. However, there are no survival cells treated with H2O because no cells are observed under the microscope. Furthermore, MTT assay is also performed to investigate the cytotoxicity of CQDAG-hydrogel to 3 T3 cells (Fig. S19). The survival rates of 3 T3 cells after incubated on the surface of the two hydrogels for 1 day are above 98%. There is an obvious increase in cell proliferation within 3 days. Besides, Live/Dead staining assay also shows that CQDAG-hydrogel have no cytotoxicity to 3 T3 cells (Fig. 5D). The cells treated with hydrogel display green color and spindle-shaped, which are the same as the cells in control group. These results indicate that the hydrogel has excellent cytocompatibility. These properties make CQDAG2-hydrogel a suitable candidate for wound healing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo inhibition of inflammation and wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: A full-thickness skin wound model is conducted on the back of rat (Fig. 6 A). 100 μL of S. aureus (1 × 109 CFU/mL) is used to infect the circular wounds with a diameter of 10 mm. Wound treated with a commercially available hydrogel dressing is served as the positive control. And CQDAG0-hydrogel group is regarded as a negative control. In this experiment, the CQDAG2-hydrogel is expected to show good antibacterial effect in vivo and wound healing ability. At the same time, it may be beneficial to skin regeneration. Fig. 6A shows the process of wound healing on 0th day, 3th day, 5th day, 7th day 10th day and 14th day. And traces of wound-bed closure during 14 days for each treatment are also drawn (Fig. 6B and 6C). On the 3th day, all the groups showed wound contraction to some extent. CQDAG2-hydrogel group display the largest wound closure rate (37%). It means that the prepared hydrogel has relatively higher promotion effect on wound healing. At 5th day, obvious epidermal regeneration occurs in wound of CQDAG2-hydrogel group. The wound closure rates of CQDAG0-hydrogel, commercial hydrogel, and CQDAG2-hydrogel are respectively 58%, 59%, and 70% (Fig. 6C). On the 7th day, the wound area of each group is further reduced. After 10 days of treatment, the healing effect of CQDAG2-hydrogel group is better than the other two groups. The corresponding wound closure rate is 98%. However, the wound closure rate in the negative control group is only 82%. On the 14th day, the wound of CQDAG2-hydrogel group is completely healed. Nevertheless, in the groups of CQDAG0-hydrogel and commercial hydrogel, there are still wounds remained in the infected sites. The above analysis of wound healing process shows that the CQDAG2-hydrogel has a better healing effect for bacterial infected skin. The results could be thanks to the potent antibacterial and antibiofilm abilities of CQDAG2-hydrogel, which would inhibit the formation of inflammation. As shown in Fig. S20, after treatment with CQDAG2-hydrogel for 24 h, there are no survival bacteria in the infected wound. However, 87% and 68% bacterial survival rates are measured in CQDAG0-hydrogel and commercial hydrogel wounds.

Furthermore, histological analysis is performed to evaluate the healing of wounds after 14 days of hydrogel treatment. Firstly, H&E is used to stain the tissues of wounds. As shown in Fig. 7 , the wounds form the basic structure of epithelium and dermis in all three cases. However, the wound of CQDAG0-hydrogel group is not completely filled. A large number of inflammatory cells aggregate in the wound site, which indicates that inflammation is not fully treated. Compared with commercial hydrogel group, CQDAG2-hydrogel group shows less inflammatory cells around the wound, which is due to the good antibacterial effect of the released CQDAG. In addition, the CQDAG2-hydrogel group exhibits higher regularity in epithelium and connective tissue, more fibroblasts, more blood vessels (indicated by red arrows) and hair follicles (indicated by blue arrows) than those in the control group. The results demonstrate that CQDAG2-hydrogel has the best healing effect.

Masson's trichrome staining of wound tissue is used to observe collagen formation. The shades of blue represent the relative content of collagen in the tissue (Fig. 8 ). The CQDAG2-hydrogel group shows higher density of collagen fiber, which is better than the results of the negative and positive control group. Furthermore, a lot of new blood vessels (indicated by red arrows) are observed in CQDAG2-hydrogel and commercial hydrogel treated wounds, indicating that the healing is sufficient. On the contrary, negative control wound shows poorer re-epithelialization, lower collagen content, and lower angiogenesis. These results display that CQDAG2-hydrogel can induce the formation of fibroblasts, the secretion and arrangement of collagen. Thus, it can accelerate the wound healing. As shown in Fig. S21, the granulation tissue of CQDAG2-hydrogel is nearly 1196.3 μm and 827.8 μm thicker than that of commercial hydrogel and CQDAG0-hydrogel group respectively. Therefore, by promoting the thickness of granulation tissue, CQDAG2-hydrogel has better wound healing and remarkable therapeutic effect than the control group. These properties, coupled with good cytocompatibility, antibiofilm property and low-drug resistance, indicate that CQDAG2-hydrogel dressing is a promising candidate for treating bacterial infections and accelerating tissue repair during wound healing.

In summary, a CQDAG antibacterial hydrogel dressing with multi-functions including antibiofilm, low-drug resistance, injectable, self-healing, stretchable and compressible properties, is successfully synthesized through the Schiff base linkage between amino groups of the CQDAG, CMCS and aldehyde groups on ODex. After decorated with CQDAG, the hydrogel possesses enhanced mechanical properties when compared with pure hydrogel. Besides, CQDAG could also promote self-healing effect of the hydrogel, which can be attributed to covalent bonds of Schiff base linkage between CQDAG and the polymer chains. Importantly, the CQDAG-hydrogel shows effectively destruction of mature S. aureus biofilm with low-drug resistance when compared to gentamicin. The acid-labile property of the Schiff base linkage enables the CQDAG to release from the hydrogel matrix in response to acidity generated by growing bacteria, which is beneficial for biofilm infection related therapy. Additionally, the CQDAG-hydrogel has good biocompatibility with non-cytotoxicity and non-hemolysis. These performances afford solid fundament for the CQDAG-hydrogel to be applied to wound dressing. In the full-thickness skin defect model, it is significantly found that the CQDAG2-hydrogel caused a faster healing process in areas of wound, coupled with a significantly reduced degree of infection in surrounding tissues. Simultaneously, the granulation tissue thickness, fibroblast density, and collagen content at the wound are all better than those of commercial hydrogel dressings. Based on these results, we believe that the prepared multifunctional CQDAG2-hydrogel is excellent candidate as an antibacterial dressing for microbial infected wound healing without concern of drug-resistance in clinical applications.
